flashcards.front: What is an object reference variable in Java?
flashcards.back: An object reference variable stores the memory address (reference) of an object rather than the object itself. It acts like a pointer to the object in heap memory.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between primitive and reference variables?
flashcards.back: Primitive variables hold actual values (int x = 5), while reference variables hold memory addresses pointing to objects (Dog d = new Dog()).

flashcards.front: What happens when you assign one reference variable to another?
flashcards.back: Both variables now point to the SAME object in memory. No new object is created - this is called aliasing. Changes through either reference affect the same object.
Aliasing occurs when two reference variables point to the same object in memory. Both references can access and modify the same object.

flashcards.front: Where are objects stored in Java memory?
flashcards.back: Objects are stored in the Heap memory, while primitive variables and reference variables are stored in the Stack memory.

flashcards.front: What is a null reference in Java?
flashcards.back: A null reference is a reference that doesn't point to any object (Object ref = null). It indicates that the variable doesn't reference any valid object.

flashcards.front: What is the default value of an object reference variable?
flashcards.back: The default value of an object reference variable is null when declared as an instance variable (class member).

flashcards.front: What is the difference between == and .equals() for objects?
flashcards.back: == compares whether two references point to the same object (memory address). .equals() compares object contents (depends on implementation).

flashcards.front: What is the difference between reference copying and object cloning?
flashcards.back: Reference copying creates two variables pointing to the same object. Object cloning creates a new independent object with copied data.
A shallow copy creates a new object but copies only primitive values and references, not nested objects. Changes to nested objects affect both original and copy.
A deep copy creates a completely independent object including all nested objects. Changes to the copy don't affect the original object.

flashcards.front: What is the 'this' keyword in Java?
flashcards.back: 'this' is a reference variable that refers to the current object. It's used to differentiate instance variables from local parameters with the same name.

flashcards.front: When is an object eligible for garbage collection in Java?
flashcards.back: An object becomes eligible for garbage collection when no more references point to it (reference count becomes zero).

flashcards.front: Can you have an array of object references?
flashcards.back: Yes, you can declare an array of object references like Dog[] dogs = new Dog[5]; Each element is initially null until assigned objects.

flashcards.front: What happens when you pass an object to a method?
flashcards.back: The object reference is passed (pass-by-value of reference), meaning the method gets a copy of the reference pointing to the same object.
